q: A changing magnetic field could produce a ___.
The discovery of electromagnetic induction was made in ___ by Michael Faraday and Joseph Henry.
The effect of electromagnetic induction is the physics behind the ___ that provide electricity in modern society.
Electric current can be classified as two types: direct current (DC) and ___ current (AC).
The direct current is a steady ___ current.
In a direct current the electrons flow steadily in the ___ direction all the time.
The current that flows in a flashlight, cell phones, laptops, and any appliance running on the batteries is ___ current.
Another type of current of great industrial importance is the ___ current.
An alternating current is a current that reverses its direction at regular ___ intervals.
In the alternating current the direction of the flow of electrons changes ___.
Current flowing in power lines and normal household electricity from a wall outlet (plug) is ___ current.
In Japan and most of America, the voltage is between 100 V and ___ V.
Europe and most other countries in the world (including Myanmar) use a voltage between ___ V and 240 V.
The frequency of AC current is 50 Hz/60 Hz in Japan, ___ Hz in US, and 50 Hz in Europe.
The waveform of the emf is called a ___ wave.
The induced emf at a particular instant is represented by $E = E_m \sin \omega t$, where $E_m$ is the maximum value of emf and $\omega$ is the angular ___ of the rotating coil.
The angular velocity $\omega$ is equal to $2 \pi f$, where f is the ___ of the emf.
The frequency f is the number of complete ___ generated in 1 s.
A rotating coil in a magnetic field is the basic of an ___ generator.
The magnitude of an alternating current at any instant is given by $I = ___ \sin \omega t$.
The alternating current is universally used for household lighting, heating, cooking and other ___ functions.
The advantage of alternating current is that it is relatively easy to change the ___ of the current.
The inevitable loss of energy is far smaller with alternating current (at high voltage and low current) than with ___ current.
When a coil of wire is rotated continuously in a magnetic field, an ___ is induced in the coil.
When a galvanometer is connected across the ends of the rotating coil, the ___ will show a peculiar result.
In each revolution, the current produced due to induced emf rises to a maximum value, then falls to zero, reverses, rises to a maximum and falls to ___ again.
The current having periodic characteristics is called ___ current.
The induced emf also shows such ___ variation.
Direct current (DC) is a steady unidirectional current, while alternating current (AC) ___ its direction at regular time intervals.
AC current is better than DC in long distance distribution of electrical power because energy loss is far smaller with alternating current at high voltage and ___ current.
In 1831, Joseph Henry and Michael Faraday independently discovered the methods of producing electric current from ___ field.
The first wire loop in Faraday's experiments is connected to a battery through a ___.
The second wire loop in Faraday's experiments is connected to a ___.
As soon as the switch is closed, a current flows through the first loop and the galvanometer gives a ___ deflection.
When the switch is kept closed, the galvanometer shows ___ deflection.
When the switch is opened again, the galvanometer shows a brief deflection in the ___ direction, indicating induced current flows in the opposite direction.
A momentary current flows through the second loop when the first loop carrying a current is moved abruptly towards or ___ from the second loop.
The same phenomenon happens when a ___, instead of a current-carrying loop, is moved abruptly towards or away from the second loop.
Switching the current in the first loop ON and OFF causes a change in the magnetic lines of force through the second loop and causes a ___ current to flow.
A current flows through a loop of wire whenever there is a change in the ___ lines of force through the loop.
The phenomenon where current flows through a loop of wire due to a change in magnetic lines of force is called ___ induction.
The current produced by electromagnetic induction is called an ___ current.
The electromotive force (emf) which causes the induced current to flow is called an ___ emf.
The magnetic flux density or magnetic induction B is the number of magnetic lines of force passing ___ through a unit area of material.
Magnetic flux density B is related to magnetic field strength (magnetic field intensity) H by $B = ___$.
The SI unit of magnetic flux density is ___ (T).
Tesla (T) is equivalent to weber per ___ squared ($Wb \ m^{-2}$).
Magnetic flux $\phi_B$ is a measurement of the total magnetic lines of force which passes through a ___ area.
In electromagnetism, the magnetic flux through a surface is the surface integral of the normal component of the magnetic field B over that ___.
Magnetic flux is given by $\phi_B = \int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A}$ where $d\vec{A}$ is the elemental ___ area.
If the magnetic field is uniform over a plane area A, the magnetic flux $\phi_B$ passing through that area A is simply $B A \cos ___$.
When the field is perpendicular to the surface area, $\theta = 0^\circ$, hence $\phi_B = ___$.
When the field is parallel to the surface area, $\theta = 90^\circ$, hence $\phi_B = ___$.
The SI unit of magnetic flux is the ___ (Wb).
The weber is named after the German physicist Wilhelm Eduard ___ (1804-1891).
Magnetic flux density B has the SI unit of ___.
Magnetic field strength H has the SI unit of ___ per metre (A $m^{-1}$).
Magnetic flux $\phi$ has the SI unit of ___ (Wb).
Magnetic field strength and magnetic flux density are ___ quantities.
Magnetic flux is a ___ quantity.
The change in magnetic flux through a wire loop produces an ___ emf.
Michael Faraday (1831) discovered the law of ___ induction.
The magnitude of induced emf in a wire loop is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic ___ through that loop.
For one turn of a wire loop, induced emf is $E = ___$.
For a wire loop with N identical turns, induced emf is $E = -N \frac{d\phi_B}{dt}$. In this equation, the minus sign gives the ___ of the induced emf.
If the wire loop is not moving (or) is moving parallel to the magnetic field lines, there is ___ induced emf (or) induced current.
Faraday's law gives the magnitude of the induced emf and ___ law gives its direction.
In 1834, the Russian physicist Heinrich Friedrich Emil Lenz stated a ___.
An induced emf is always in a direction that ___ the change in the original magnetic flux that causes it.
The direction of induced current can be found by applying ___ law.
Lenz's law is a good example of the principle of ___ of energy.
Lenz's law shows how mechanical work done against the opposing force is transformed into ___ energy.
When a magnet is moved towards the coil, the magnetic flux through the coil ___.
The induced current in the coil must produce a magnetic field which opposes an increase in the original ___ flux.
When the N pole of the magnet is moved towards the coil, the end of the coil nearer the magnet must act as a ___ pole.
When the N pole of the magnet is moved towards the coil, the current in the coil must be flowing in a ___ clockwise direction if viewed from the magnet.
If the magnet is moved away from the coil, the induced current will flow in the ___ direction.
One useful application of electromagnetic induction is an induction ___.
In 1851, Heinrich Ruhmkorff produced an electric spark 30 cm long using an ___ coil.
An induction coil consists of a core of soft ___ wires.
Around the soft iron core is wrapped the ___ coil, made of a few turns of thick insulated wire.
Around the primary coil is wound the ___ coil, which has many turns of thin insulated wire.
The primary coil of an induction coil is connected to a ___.
When the switch S is pressed, a current flows through the primary, and an induced ___ develops in every turn of the secondary.
As current flows through the primary, the soft iron core becomes an ___.
The electromagnet attracts a soft iron ___ mounted on the spring.
When the platinum contacts are separated, the current ___ flowing.
When the primary current stops, a very high induced emf develops in the ___ again.
When the primary is connected to a 6 V battery, a voltage of about ___ V can develop across the terminals A and B.
A capacitor is connected across the platinum contacts to prevent sparking and ___ away of contacts.
The induction coil is used in the ___ system of motor cars.
A generator is an electromagnetic device which converts ___ energy into electrical energy.
AC generators are also called ___.
AC generators work on the principle of Faraday's law of electromagnetic ___.
A simple AC generator consists of a rectangular coil mounted on an ___.
The induced current in an AC generator flows through two ___ rings (S1 and S2).
The slip rings in an AC generator make sliding contact with two fixed carbon ___ (B1 and B2).
Every time the coil in an AC generator turns through 180°, the current ___ its direction.
If the speed of the rotating coil is doubled, both the frequency and the rate of cutting of magnetic lines of force will be ___.
If the speed of the rotating coil is doubled, the maximum output voltage is also ___.
When the number of turns in the coil is doubled, the frequency of the output voltage is the same although its magnitude is ___.
The induced emf of a generator can be increased by increasing the ___ of rotation of the coil.
The induced emf of a generator can be increased by increasing the ___ of the coil.
The induced emf of a generator can be increased by increasing the ___ of turns in the coil.
The induced emf of a generator can be increased by using stronger ___.
If the wire loop is rotating with a constant angular velocity $\omega$, the induced emf is $E = NBA \omega \sin \omega t = ___ \sin \omega t$.
In an electric motor, electrical energy is used to rotate the coil to provide ___ energy.
In generators, mechanical energy is used to rotate the coil to produce ___ energy.
Transformers are used to provide different ___ requirements of appliances from the mains supply.
A transformer is used to change the ___ of an alternating current.
The core of a transformer is built up of thin iron sheets of high resistance, called ___.
The iron sheets in a transformer core are made to be electrically ___ from one another.
One coil connected to an alternating voltage source is called the ___ coil.
The coil connected to an electrical device in a transformer is called the ___ coil.
When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, an alternating current produces an alternating ___ flux in the core.
This changing magnetic flux produces an induced emf and an induced current in the ___ coil.
The voltage in the secondary and the primary have the same ___.
Laminated iron (mumetal) core is used to minimize power loss due to ___ current flowing in the core.
Power loss due to heating of wire used in the coils occurs when ___ flows through them.
In a transformer, power input is assumed to be equal to power ___.
The product of voltage and current is the same on both coils of a transformer, so $V_p I_p = ___$.
A transformer which increases the voltage will ___ the current in the same proportion.
In an ideal transformer, the emf in each coil is proportional to the number of ___ in the coil.
The turns ratio is given by $\frac{N_s}{N_p}$ where $N_s$ is secondary turns and $N_p$ is primary ___.
In an ideal transformer, with no power losses, $E_p = V_p$ and $E_s = ___$.
The combined relationship for an ideal transformer is $\frac{E_s}{E_p} = \frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = ___$.
If $V_s > V_p$ (secondary voltage is greater than primary), the transformer is called a ___ transformer.
If $V_s < V_p$ (secondary voltage is smaller than primary), the transformer is called a ___ transformer.
Currents flowing in little circles in the iron core itself due to changing magnetic field are called ___ currents.
The efficiency of a transformer is the ratio of output power to ___ power.
Most transformers have full load efficiency from 95% to ___%.
No ideal transformer exists with ___% efficiency due to power losses.
To improve transformer efficiency, soft ___ material should be used to make the core.
A laminated core should be used to reduce the flow of ___ current.
Low-resistance copper wires should be used as coils to reduce energy loss in the form of ___.
Transformers work on AC, but not on ___.
Unless there is a changing current in the input coil, no voltage is induced in the ___ coil.
A transformer connected to a DC supply can be damaged as the high current flowing through the input coil can make it ___.
The electricity generated at the power station is transmitted to the consumers through the power ___.
A generator produces 10 MW of electrical power at ___ kV.
Power can be calculated using the formula $P = ___$.
If electrical power is transmitted through power cables, electrical power is lost as ___ in the transmission line.
Power loss is one of the main problems in the long distance ___ of electricity.
One way to reduce power loss is to use very thick cables of low ___.
A more practical way to reduce power loss is to reduce the magnitude of the transmission current by stepping up the ___.
When the voltage is increased from 10 kV to 20 kV, the power loss will be reduced to ___ times.
The national ___ system is used to distribute electricity around the country.
An advantage of the grid system is that power stations in areas of low demand can supply areas where demand is ___.
An alternating current is generated at high voltage (e.g., 10 kV) from the ___ station.
The voltage is then stepped up (e.g., ___ kV) for transmission.
The voltage is finally stepped down at the ___ in stages before it is used in factories and homes.
Power distribution through a grid system can be carried out with the help of ___.
Electricity supplied to the house is a single-phase form of ___ current (AC).
The thick cable carrying electricity to the house contains two wires which are well ___ from each other.
One of the two wires in house wiring is the ___ wire (L).
The other wire in house wiring is the ___ wire (N).
The live wire (L) is a dangerous wire as it carries a ___ voltage.
The neutral wire (N) is usually at ___ volt.
Electricity is fed into the house through the main fuse, meter, main switch and then to the ___ box.
From the distribution box, there are normally three circuits: lighting, power (ring main), and ___ circuits.
The new colour code for wiring uses brown for live, blue for neutral, and striped ___ for earth.
The old colour code for wiring used red for live, black for neutral, and ___ for earth.
In the lighting circuit, the light bulbs are arranged in ___ across the mains line.
Since bulbs are connected in parallel, other bulbs are not affected when one bulb is switched ON or ___.
If bulbs are connected in series, switching OFF one bulb would switch ___ all the others.
The lighting circuit has only two wires: live and ___.
A lighting circuit must be connected sequentially as live wire, fuse, switch, load and then to ___ wire.
The power sockets in a house are connected by means of a ___ circuit.
In a ring main circuit, the live, neutral, and earth wires form a ___ of cable.
Power circuits are for appliances that consume ___ power than heating appliances.
Since current can flow to any particular socket by two ways in a ring main circuit, ___ wires can be used.
An earth wire (E) is added to the ring main circuit for ___ reasons.
Each main ring circuit uses a ___ A fuse.
___-pin sockets and three-pin plugs are used in the ring main circuit.
Heating circuits are for appliances such as electric cookers, ovens, hot plates, washing machines and ___.
Appliances connected in heating circuits use relatively ___ amounts of power.
Each fuse used in a heating circuit is rated at ___ A.
Circuits must be cared for not to be ___.
The fuse of each circuit must be connected in the ___ (L) lead.
A fuse connected in the neutral lead is ___.
A circuit breaker is an automatic switch which ___ (turns OFF) when the current rises above the specified value.
A circuit breaker can be reset by turning the switch ON or pressing a ___.
Earthing of an electrical appliance is the most important safety ___.
An earthing system connects specific parts of an electric power system with the ___.
In any electrical appliance, the ___ wire is always connected to the metal case.
If the live wire becomes loose and touches the metal body, the case of the iron would become ___ at high voltage.
With proper earthing, the current would pass directly into the earth through the earth wire, which has much smaller ___ than human being.
Lamps are connected in parallel in the lighting circuit so that the other bulbs are not affected when one bulb is switched ___ or OFF.
Fuses and switches are placed on the ___ wire to ensure the circuit is made safe by cutting off the high voltage supply.
The lighting circuit is the appropriate wiring circuit for ___ and fluorescent lamps.
The heating circuit is the appropriate wiring circuit for an electric ___.
The heating circuit is the appropriate wiring circuit for a water pump ___.
The heating circuit is the appropriate wiring circuit for an electric ___.
Electrical faults in appliances or circuits can cause fires, electric shocks, as well as ___ to users.
Either high current or high ___ can be dangerous to human.
The dangers of electricity can be traced to three possible causes: damaged insulation, overheating of cables, and ___ conditions.
All electrical appliances require two wires, the live and ___ wires, to form a complete circuit.
The live wire is the dangerous wire because it carries a ___ voltage.
The neutral wire is close to ___ voltage.
Live and neutral wires are insulated in ___ rubber.
Insulating materials ___ with time and use.
Damaged electrical insulation can cause the electrical insulation to ___ and break.
If the vulcanised rubber covering the live wire is damaged, the exposed live wire can cause a severe ___ to the user.
A severe electric shock can lead to serious injury and even ___.
The overheating of cables is due to the extremely large current flowing under conditions such as a short circuit or ___.
A short circuit can result when the live wire makes electrical contact with the neutral wire due to ___ insulation.
A short circuit produces a large current and large amount of heat, which can melt insulation and start a ___.
Overuse of extension cords and multiple plug adapters on the same circuit are typical causes of an electrical ___.
Overload occurs when an equipment draws an ___ current from the supply.
Many electrical accidents occur in ___ conditions in a wet bathroom.
If water from the wet floor touches the live wire, it provides a conducting path for current, exposing the person to the danger of ___.
The human body can only withstand up to about ___ mA of current.
The current will be much higher due to the sharp decrease in the resistance of the ___ body.
Other possible hazards include using hair dryers, electric irons or changing a light bulb with ___ hands.
A current of 1 mA corresponds to the Threshold effect, where there is ___ pain.
A current of 5 mA causes a frightening but ___ shock.
A current of 10-20 mA causes uncontrolled muscular ___.
A current of 50 mA causes pain and exhaustion, with ___ affected.
A current of 100-300 mA causes uncoordinated contraction of the heart leading to ___.
One should never operate an electrical appliance with ___ hands.
Switches, plugs, sockets, connecting wires, etc. should always be in a ___ condition.
The amount of current flowing through the body determines whether you receive an electrical ___.
Overheating of cables is caused by extremely large current flowing due to a short circuit or ___.
